entry.title: USB 3D Gauss Meter
entry.summary: A USB 3D-Gauss Meter based on Texas Instrument's TMAG5170 Hall Sensor.
entry.content:
This project is a USB 3D-Gauss Meter based on Texas Instrument's TMAG5170 Hall Sensor. The Gauss Meter is powered by an STM32, which sends the measured data through USB. There is a Python GUI client to show and log the measurements on your computer screen.

Python GUI client setup

First of all, you can download all the design files and the STM32 firmware and Python GUI client from Github.

git clone https://github.com/light655/USB-3D-Gauss-Meter.git

The Python GUI client requires Pyside6 and pyserial packages to run. You can install them with pip.

pip3 install -r python-client/requirements.txt

Then, you can run the Python script to start the GUI client. There are currently two versions of firmware and Python GUI client. Please use version two.

python3 python-client/gauss-meter-v2.py

Python GUI client usage

First, select the serial device. The USB 3D Gauss Meter should show up as STM32 Virtual ComPort. Next, you can select the measurement range. There are three options: 75mT, 150mT and 300mT.

If you want to log to measured data, please check the "Save results" checkbox and select a file to save to. By default, logging will stop after 1000 samples are taken. If you want longer logging length, you can enter the number of samples that you wish to log. There is no limitation to the number of logged samples, as long as your hard drive can fit the data. Next, select a sampling rate. In the current version, the maximum logging sampling rate is 2500 samples per second.

In logging mode, the program will produce a CSV file. There are four columns in the CSV file: time and magnetic field on the three axes. The units are microseconds (us) and millitesla (mT) for time and magnetic field, respectively.

Warning

The selected sampling rate is only an estimation. Slight difference in sampling rate is expected due to device variation of the TMAG5170 Hall sensor. Please use the timestamp provided by the logging firmware for more precise timing.

To start logging or just displaying measured magnetic field on the screen, click the "Start" button. The program will stop logging when the set number of measurements is reached. In display mode, the TMAG5170 is set to 32x averaging mode and a measurement is taken around every 0.5 seconds. To force stop and change settings, you can click the "Reset" button.

STM32 firmware

There are two versions of firmware currently. Please use version two firmware with version two Python client. The USB 3D Gauss Meter is loaded with the version two firmware when shipped. You need not flash it yourself.

However, if you decide to hack around with the firmware (you are very welcomed to do so), you can upload it via the SWD headers. You can use any SWD compatible debug probes, such as Raspberry Pi Picoprobe to program the STM32. The SWD headers are arranged in a zigzag footprint. This way, you can insert pin headers without soldering.

The current firmware cannot run at the maximum speed of the TMAG5170 Hall effect sensor. I suspect this is due to the use of C++ in the firmware.

Links

  • Github repository containing the hardware design, STM32 firmware and Python client.

entry.title: TPS25200 eFuse Module
entry.summary: An electronic, re-settable fuse module to protect your circuit from drawing too much current.
entry.content:
It is always nice to have some protection against over current or short circuit situations. The TPS25200 eFuse Module offers programmable over current protection for your circuit. When the current draw exceeds the current-limit threshold for more than 8ms, the eFuse will cut the current. The eFuse can be reset by a push button.

Features

  • Programmable current limit up to 2.9A
  • 60mΩ(typical) MOSFET switch
  • Automatic startup(requires fast rising input, see datasheet)
  • 8ms deglitch
  • 3~5V input

The current-limit threshold is programmed via a potentiometer, ranging from 100mA to 2.9A. The eFuse will shutdown after an over-current event and a red LED will light up to indicate a fault. By pushing the button on the module, the eFuse will reset and resume normal operation if the over current/short is removed.

Downloads

  • Datasheet

Links

  • Purchase on Tindie

entry.title: Breadboard Hexadecimal Display
entry.summary: It always bothers be to connect a row of LEDs and current limiting resistors on a breadboard. Therefore, I came up with the idea of making a display module to display 8-bit buses on a breadboard. Also, sometimes it makes life a lot easier if the bus data is displayed in hexadecimal. In the end, I decided to make a 8-bit hexadecimal display that will fit on a breadboard.
entry.content:
This module is based on a Holtek HT66F3185 microcontroller. I've programmed two modes for this module, which is selectable by a solder jumper on the back of the module. The most significant bit of the inputs can be either on the left or on the right, depending on the mode selected.

Downloads

  • Datasheet

Links

  • Purchase on Tindie

entry.title: Split Rail Breadboard Power Supply
entry.summary: In the past, I always avoided projects that require split rail power because of the extra effort needed to get that negative rail. To solve this problem, I designed a simple +5V to -5V switching converter PCB to provide a negative rail from USB. Once connected to power via USB Type-C, the right power rail of the breadboard will be +5V and the left power rail will be -5V.
entry.content:
Features

  • USB Type-C connector!
  • Up to 250mA on the -5V rail

This module is designed to sit on top of a breadboard, providing power from USB C for your breadboard projects. There are times that a -5V rail isn't needed, the left power rail can be selected to output +5V instead. By changing the setting of the jumper, the left power rail can be either +5V or -5V. Also, there are red LED and green LED to indicate the active voltage is -5V and +5V, respectively. For more information and testing data, please refer to the datasheet.

Downloads

  • Datasheet

Links

  • Purchase on Tindie

entry.title: PMOD 16MB Flash Module
entry.summary: A 16MB Winbond W25Q128JVSIQ flash module with PMOD interface that can be easily connected to an FPGA.
entry.content:
The PMOD 16MB Flash module is a breakout board specifically designed to connect to an FPGA development board. This module has a PMOD interface, which is supported on various FPGA boards. All pins on the W25Q128JVSIQ flash chip is broken out onto the headers and pull-up resistors are added to disable write protection and chip reset by default.

This module comes in handy when you need to add storage to your FPGA projects. In fact, I designed this module for my FPGA lab final project in college! Besides FPGA, I also designed the module to be compatible with breadboards. There are 2 additional pins on the outer row so that all pins can be accessed on a breadboard.

Downloads

  • Datasheet

Links

  • Purchase on Tindie

entry.title: Pico Debug Carrier
entry.summary: The RP2040 chip designed by Raspberry Pi has many features including the ability to emulate a SWD interface and act as the debugger for other ARM microcontrollers. Connecting two Picos together creates a mess of wires, so I came up with the idea of designing an RP2040-based board to take the job of the second of the second Pico and get rid of the messy wiring.
entry.content:
With the Pico Debug Carrier, we can solder on the target Pico and use the Picoprobe firmware written by Raspberry Pi to debug the target Pico. All of the circuitry is squeeze onto a PCB the same size as an Arduino Uno and accessed by an USB type C port. Although the target Pico is soldered onto the PCB, the Pico Debug Carrier still provides full access of the pins on the Pico through pin headers.

The Pico Debug Carrier is designed to be a 2-in-1 development kit. Since debugging the Pico only requires 4 GPIO pins, the rest of the GPIOs are broken out, so that this board can also be used as a normal development board. The PCB is designed to match the form factor of the Arduino Uno, and the position of the special pins(I2C, UART) are rearranged to be at same place as the Arduino Uno.

There are a few improvements in terms of development board to the Pi Pico.

  • A reset button is added, we no longer need to short the RUN pin to ground with wires to reset the microcontroller.
  • There is 16MB of flash. Although 2MB is plenty for the Picoprobe firmware, a larger flash chip is used just in case.
  • There is a USB-C connector. No more up-side-down USB connectors!
  • There are 2 user LEDs connected to GPIOs 24 and 25.

Downloads

  • Datasheet

Links

  • Purchase on Tindie
  • Video tutorial on how to solder the targe Pico onto the Pico Debug Carrier

entry.title: 8-bit register with LEDs based on 74HC273
entry.summary: The 8-bit register breakout board shrinks the 74HC273 D-flip-flop IC and LEDs onto a PCB only a little larger than a DIP-20 package. All pins on the 74HC273 is broken out and a pull-up resistor is added to the clear pin to prevent the device resetting itself.
entry.content:
This breakout board is ideal for building breadboard logic circuits, we can look at the signals stored in the register without using a bunch of breadboard space on 5mm LEDs and resistors. During the design process, I altered the pin arrangement of the breakout board. Instead of following the pinout of the 74HC273 IC, I moved the pins around so that the outputs and inputs are in order from 0 to 7 on each side.

Downloads

  • Datasheet

Links

  • Purchase on Tindie
